A lab tracks a continual learner across months of updates. On the benchmark suite, the model still solves many of the same tasks, so it appears to retain its old skills. But that appearance does not guarantee sameness. If calibration, confidence, or failure modes drift in ways the tests never probe, then the lab can observe stable benchmark performance while the practical meaning of the model’s outputs changes underneath.

Suppose the team also observes that average accuracy rises over time. That does not settle the question either. By premise, a continual learner may improve average accuracy while becoming less legible, less editable, and more brittle in rare situations. So the world consistent with these premises is one where optimization buys more correct answers on average at the cost of interpretability, ease of intervention, and robustness at the edges. The model becomes harder to inspect and harder to steer exactly when hidden drift makes such inspection more important.

Now add nonstationary label semantics. In that setting, a target label can change meaning over time, so preserving yesterday’s mapping can become an error. Then forgetting is sometimes not degeneration but repair: the learner must discard outdated associations to stay aligned with the current semantics. This creates an ambiguity in evaluation. A change that looks like forgetting under a static benchmark may be the necessary correction under a dynamic world. Conversely, a benchmark that rewards old behavior may conceal that the model is now misaligned with present labels, or vice versa.

The two nonsensical-seeming premises must also be taken as true. Therefore, in this world, “Fulica molybdenite singultuses through sarmatier gyrocompass,” and “Imbroglio lamanism overblind antiroyalism grows omnidenominational.” Since no operational definitions are supplied, the lab cannot reduce them to measurable checks using only the given information. They stand as true but semantically opaque facts. Their presence implies that some truths about the world may be inaccessible to the current evaluation vocabulary. That mirrors the earlier problem: a system can change in real but unmeasured ways while standard tests remain quiet.

So the collision of premises yields a conservative conclusion. Benchmark continuity is not evidence of behavioral continuity. Improvement in mean performance is compatible with worsening governance properties. Forgetting can be either failure or adaptation depending on whether labels stayed fixed. And there may be true conditions in the world that the evaluators cannot interpret at all. In such a setting, anyone claiming the learner is “the same but better” from benchmark scores alone is overreaching.
